stem: An email spam filter classifies emails as spam or not-spam. Historical data shows that 20% of all incoming emails are actually spam. The filter correctly identifies spam 92% of the time (sensitivity) and correctly identifies non-spam 88% of the time (specificity). However, the true proportion of spam emails fluctuates daily, and the IT department estimates it could be anywhere from 15% to 25% on a given day. An email is flagged as spam by the filter. What is the probability it is actually spam?

ground_truth.answer: P(Spam | Flagged) ranges from 0.575 to 0.719 as the spam proportion varies from 15% to 25%. Point estimate at 20%: 0.657.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(Spam|Flag) = P(Flag|Spam)*P(Spam) / [P(Flag|Spam)*P(Spam) + P(Flag|¬Spam)*P(¬Spam)].
common_failure_modes: Answering with the sensitivity (92%) instead of computing the posterior
Providing only the point estimate without reflecting the daily fluctuation in spam rate
Failing to account for the false positive rate from non-spam emails
